_meta.description: Quarterly tax installment rules for self-employed workers in Quebec and federally. One of the most common financial surprises for first-year self-employed people.

title_en: Quarterly Tax Installments for Self-Employed Workers
plain_language_summary: When you're employed, your employer deducts taxes from every paycheque so you never owe a big lump sum. When you're self-employed, nobody withholds tax for you. If you'll owe more than a certain threshold, the government requires you to pay estimated taxes quarterly throughout the year — not just in April. Missing these payments means interest charges. Many first-year self-employed people are caught off guard by this.

the_surprise.title: Why this catches people off guard
the_surprise.explanation: In year 1, most self-employed people don't know about installments and don't pay them. They face a large tax bill in April plus interest. In year 2, the CRA and Revenu Québec send installment notices — but by then the habit of saving for taxes should already be established.
the_surprise.action: From your first month of self-employment: set aside 30-35% of every payment you receive in a separate savings account. This covers income tax + QPP + QPIP.

federal_installments.threshold: You must pay installments if your net tax owing exceeds $3,000 in the current year AND in either of the two previous years
federal_installments.first_year_exception: In your first year of self-employment, installments are technically not required if you didn't owe $3,000 in either of the two prior years — but you'll owe it all in April, so saving quarterly is still essential

how_much_to_pay.option_1: Prior year method: Pay 1/4 of last year's tax owing each quarter
how_much_to_pay.option_2: Current year estimate: Estimate your current year tax and pay 1/4 quarterly
how_much_to_pay.option_3: No-calculation method: CRA tells you how much based on your prior 2 years
how_much_to_pay.recommendation: In year 1, use current year estimate. Once you have prior year data, the prior year method is simplest.

how_to_pay: Online banking (CRA is a payee at most Canadian banks)
By mail (cheque payable to Receiver General of Canada)
federal_installments.interest_rate: CRA charges prescribed interest rate + 4% on late installments (approximately 8-10% in 2026 — verify current rate)

quebec_installments.threshold: You must pay Quebec installments if your net Quebec tax owing exceeds $1,800 in the current year AND in either of the two previous years
quebec_installments.note: Quebec's threshold is lower than federal ($1,800 vs $3,000) — you may need to pay Quebec installments before triggering the federal requirement
how_to_pay: Online banking (Revenu Québec is a payee at Quebec banks)
By mail (cheque payable to Minister of Revenue of Quebec)

practical_calculation.title: How much should I set aside each month?

components.name: Federal income tax
components.approximate_rate: 15-26% of net income depending on bracket

components.name: Quebec income tax (after abatement)
components.approximate_rate: 14-19% of net income for most micro-entrepreneurs

practical_calculation.rule_of_thumb: Set aside 30% of net income for taxes and contributions if you earn under $50,000. Set aside 35-40% if you earn $50,000-$100,000.

examples.note: At $50K net, QPP + higher tax brackets push the rate up

installment_reminders.cra_notices: CRA sends installment reminder notices in February and August — these tell you the amount based on prior year. You are NOT required to follow these exact amounts — they're based on last year and may be wrong for your current year.
installment_reminders.revenu_quebec_notices: Revenu Québec sends similar notices. Same principle applies.
installment_reminders.important: Installment reminder notices are not bills — they're estimates. If your income is lower this year, you can pay less. If higher, pay more. You true up in April.

penalties_and_interest.interest_only: Unlike late filing, missed installments don't result in penalties — only interest charges on the shortfall
penalties_and_interest.interest_starts: Interest starts accruing from the day the installment was due
penalties_and_interest.offset: If you overpay in one quarter and underpay in another, the overpayment offsets the interest
penalties_and_interest.practical_note: In year 1, if you genuinely didn't know about installments, the interest charge is usually manageable. In year 2+, there's no excuse — budget for it.

common_mistakes: Not saving throughout the year and being hit with a $8,000+ bill in April — the most common first-year mistake
Thinking the installment reminder notice is optional — it's required if you meet the threshold
Using the CRA's prior-year installment amounts when your income has dropped significantly — you can pay less based on your current year estimate
Forgetting that Quebec installments and federal installments are separate payments to separate agencies
Not taking the Quebec $1,800 threshold seriously — it's lower than federal and kicks in sooner
